title: The Sequence Approach (8 sequences)
author: Paul Joseph Gulino
source: Screenwriting: The Sequence Approach, 2004. A legacy of silent-era practice of shipping films on physical reels.

lineage.note: Argues the three-act model is too coarse and that films are really built from eight 10-15 minute sequences, each a mini-movie with its own tension and partial resolution. The most useful corrective to 'what do I do in the sagging middle of Act II.'

stages.name: Sequence A — Status quo + inciting incident
stages.function: Establish protagonist and world; the inciting incident lands.

stages.name: Sequence B — Predicament and lock-in
stages.function: Protagonist commits to a course of action; the new direction is locked in.

stages.name: Sequence C — First obstacle, raising stakes
stages.function: Initial attempt to solve the problem fails; stakes raised.

stages.name: Sequence D — First culmination / midpoint
stages.function: A second, partial culmination — what looked like the goal turns out to be something else, or the real problem becomes visible.

stages.name: Sequence E — Subplot and rising action
stages.function: Subplot intensifies; the protagonist tries new tactics.

stages.name: Sequence F — Main culmination, end of Act II
stages.function: Major setback / all is lost / point of decision.

stages.name: Sequence G — New tension and twist
stages.function: Act III opens with new conditions; final approach to the climax.

applicability.limitations: Requires identifying eight clear local tension/resolution arcs; if the material has only three or four, forcing eight feels artificial.
